memory.summary: Teammate_A is asking me to take over their part because they have an emergency, and since I'm better at it anyway, I should probably just do it so we don't have a problem.
I took over teammate_A's work to avoid conflict and help the team, but I feel drained now. My stress is rising even though I wanted to keep things smooth.
I realized I could have pushed back without hurting our trust, but my fear of friction made me just say yes again.
I absorbed their work to keep the peace, but now I'm exhausted and realize my 'help' just made me carry the burden alone.

I swallowed the conflict to keep things smooth, but now both our deadlines are missed and I'm left carrying the weight alone.
I agreed to their request just to keep the peace, but now my own deadline is tighter and I'm exhausted. My avoidance of conflict has only made things worse for both of us.
I just made an excuse to buy time, but my teammate is still waiting for a real plan. My short-term relief feels like I'm digging a deeper hole for us both.

desires: Stop feeling like the only one who has to carry the mess so I don't have to face the awkwardness of telling them it's too much.
Get a quick win or a distraction before my brain completely shuts down from exhaustion.
Make sure teammate_A doesn't think I'm incompetent just because I'm avoiding the hard conversation about their deadline.

goals.description: Reply to teammate_A's panic message with a vague 'let's figure it out at dinner' deflection, then immediately close the laptop and join cohort_3 before I have to think about the actual code.
goals.rationale: The urge to fix this alone is too heavy right now; my affiliation drive makes facing their frustration feel like a personal rejection. My avoidant style means I'd rather pretend we'll solve it together over food than admit I'm stuck. Impulsivity dictates that staying here any longer will just make me more anxious, so fleeing to the group meal is the only way to regulate my stress.

goals.description: Eat whatever everyone else is eating at the table without checking my phone, just to prove I'm still part of the tribe even if the project is failing.
goals.rationale: Food with friends is my primary anchor for feeling safe. If I don't physically put myself in the middle of the group dynamic, the guilt of the unfinished work might eat me alive. I need the sensory distraction of the meal to override the cognitive load of the deadline.

agent.last_reasoning: My affiliation drive makes facing their panic feel like rejection; avoiding friction is safer than admitting I can't handle the dumped work. Impulsivity says fleeing to cohort_3 for food is the only way to regulate stress now. This vague deflection preserves closeness while letting me escape immediately.

goals.description: Say a vague, non-committal 'maybe' to teammate_A's request without actually agreeing or explaining my stress, just to keep the peace for now.
goals.rationale: As an Affiliation/Avoidant type, keeping teammate_A happy and avoiding immediate awkwardness outweighs my actual workload. I'll dodge the hard 'no' to preserve closeness, even though it adds risk later.

goals.description: Grind through my own project tasks frantically until 7 PM so I don't look like a failure tomorrow.
goals.rationale: My Impulsive nature means I'm reacting to the looming deadline rather than planning ahead. I need to finish *something* before the panic hits, but I'm likely underestimating how long this will take because I can't ask for help or sleep.

desires: Keep teammate_A happy and avoid the awkwardness of saying no or admitting I'm overwhelmed.
